title: Campbell penetration depth in a single crystal of heavy fermion superconductor CeCoIn$_5$
authors: Hyunsoo Kim, Makariy A. Tanatar, Cedomir Petrovic, Ruslan Prozorov
abstract: The temperature and magnetic field dependent magnetic penetration depth, $λ_m(T,H)$, was measured in a single crystal of a heavy fermion superconductor CeCoIn$_5$ using a frequency-domain tunnel diode resonator. In addition to the London penetration depth, which yields the superfluid density, measurements in a finite DC magnetic field provide Campbell penetration depth, $λ_C(T,H)$, which is directly linked to the true (unrelaxed) critical current density, $J_c$. The measured $λ_C(H)$ in CeCoIn$_5$ deviates significantly from the conventional $\sim \sqrt{H}$ behavior, and its slope changes abruptly at the characteristic magnetic field values. Considering that our sample is in the clean limit, we interpret this deviation as a fingerprint of the vortex lattice symmetry change. The temperature dependence $J_c(T)$ of CeCoIn$_5$ calculated from $λ_C(T)$ is nearly $T$-linear over the entire temperature range, also in stark contrast to expectations in a conventional type-II superconductor. Our results provide new evidence for unconventional superconductivity in CeCoIn$_5$ from the never-before-measured Campbell penetration depth.
summary: This paper uses the tunnel diode resonator technique to study the Campbell penetration depth in the heavy fermion superconductor CeCoIn5. The researchers found that the magnetic field dependence of the Campbell depth deviates from standard models, which they interpret as a change in the symmetry of the vortex lattice. Additionally, they discovered that the critical current density follows a T-linear dependence, which is highly unusual for conventional superconductors. These findings provide significant new evidence for the unconventional nature of superconductivity in this material.
main_problem: Investigating the superconducting pairing symmetry and vortex lattice properties of the heavy fermion superconductor CeCoIn5 by measuring the Campbell penetration depth.
main_result: The measured Campbell penetration depth deviates from conventional sqrt(H) behavior, signaling a vortex lattice symmetry change, and the critical current density Jc(T) shows a nearly T-linear dependence, providing new evidence for unconventional superconductivity.
method: Frequency-domain tunnel diode resonator (TDR) technique used to measure temperature and magnetic field dependent magnetic penetration depth.
model_or_system: Single crystals of the heavy fermion superconductor CeCoIn5 in the clean limit, using a single-vortex pinning model with a quadratic pinning potential.
key_observables: Magnetic penetration depth (lambda_m), Campbell penetration depth (lambda_C), London penetration depth (lambda_L), and critical current density (Jc).
important_parameters: Temperature range 0.15 K to 2.5 K, magnetic fields up to 5 T, coherence length xi(0) = 24 nm, and London penetration depth lambda_L(0) approx 260 nm.
main_assumptions: The pinning potential radius rp is assumed to be the coherence length xi, and the system is assumed to be in the clean limit.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows temperature-dependent penetration depth, its derivative, and the resulting H-T phase diagram; Fig 4 compares the temperature dependence of Jc in CeCoIn5 to other superconductors like LiFeAs and SrPd2Ge2.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scientific problem of CeCoIn5 symmetry, describes the TDR experimental setup and sample preparation, presents measurements of lambda_m and lambda_C, analyzes the vortex lattice symmetry changes and Jc temperature dependence, and concludes with a comparison to other superconducting systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Temperature-dependent in-plane magnetic penetration depth λm(T) in a single crystal CeCoIn5 sample with Hac and Hdc applied along the crystallographic c-axis. Different curves correspond to different values of the applied dc magnetic field indicated in the legend. (b) First temperature-derivative dλm/dT of λm(T) shown in panel (a). The sharp peaks correspond to superconducting phase transitions for each applied field. (c) The superconducting H-T phase diagram. The derivative maximum criteria was compared to bulk measurements determined at a discontinuous jump in dc magnetization measurements by T. Tayama et al. [51].

Figure 1(a) shows the magnetic field evolution of λm(T) from µ0Hdc = 0 to 5 T. In the absence of a magnetic field, the single crystal sample exhibits a step- like superconducting phase transition near T = 2.3 K where the London penetration depth, λL, diverges. The temperature dependence of λL in the Meissner state of unconventional superconductors is determined by the symmetry of the superconducting gap. In particular, a power-law behavior ∆λ(T) ∼T n best describes its low- temperature behavior below typically 0.3Tc. For a line- nodal superconductor, the exponent n varies between 1 and 2 depending on the impurity scattering [74] and the nature of the scattering center [75]. The...
FIG. 2. (a) Temperature-variation of Campbell penetration depth λC at various fixed fields in CeCoIn5. (b) Isothermal field-variation of λC at various fixed temperatures.

FIG. 4. Temperature-dependent theoretical critical current density Jc(t) with a dc magnetic field µ0H0 = 0.1 T in CeCoIn5 where t is the reduced temperature T/Tc. Jc(T) in LiFeAs and SrPd2Ge2 are shown for comparison, which are taken in µ0H0 = 1 T and 0.02 T, respectively. The magnetic fields for each superconductor are the minimum applied field for the measurement of Jc.
